majorEvents.notes: American Revolution
Ratification of the Declaration of Independence
Ratification of the Constitution of the United States
Presidency of George Washington
French Revolution
War of 1812 against the United Kingdom
Missouri Compromise signed into law
Indian Removal Act signed into law
Texas Revolution
Mexican-American War
California Gold Rush
Passage of the Fugitive Slave Act
First National Women's Rights Convention in Worcester, Massassachusetts
John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry, Virginia
American Civil War
Executive issuance of the Emancipation Proclamation
Ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment, abolishing slavery
Formation of the First Ku Klux Klan
Great Railroad Strike of 1877
Chinese Exclusion Act signed into law
Decision on Civil Rights Cases, allowing Jim Crow Laws
Signing of the Dawes Act, alloting land for Native American reservations
Signing of the Sherman Antitrust Act
Overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom
Start of the Panic of 1893
Decision on Plessy v. Ferguson, allowing "separate but equal" policy
William Jennings Bryan delivers Cross of Gold speech
Spanish-American War
Philippine–American War
Mexican Revolution
World War I
Red Summer, year of racial violence and union strikes
Great Influenza epidemic
Ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment; the right to vote shall not be denied on account of sex
Scopes Trial, on evolution
Execution of Sacco and Vanzetti
Teapot Dome Scandal
Black Thursday, starting the Great Depression
Franklin Roosevelt's First Hundred Days, precursor to the New Deal
World War II
Passage of the Johnson-Reed Act, barring immigration from Asia and Eastern Europe, Jews among their number
Atomic bombing of Hiroshima, introducing nuclear weapons
The Bracero Program, bringing in Mexican migrants to work in the United States
Internment of Japanese Americans
Cold War Red Scare, wherein individuals were investigated for Communist influence, beginning with Truman's Executive Order 9835 and ending with decision of Yates v. United States
US involvement in Vietnam War
Apollo 11 spaceflight; first moon landing
Decision on Brown v. Board of Education, outlawing "separate but equal" policies
Birmingham campaign; coordinated movement aiming at desegregation
Civil Rights Act signed into law
Kent State shootings
Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

authorGroups.name: Golden Age of Indiana Literature
authorGroups.description: The Golden Age of Indiana Literature is a period from 1880 to 1920 when many nationally and internationally acclaimed literary works were created by natives of the state of Indiana.

authorGroups.name: Fireside poets
authorGroups.description: The fireside poets – also known as the schoolroom or household poets – were a group of 19th-century American poets associated with New England.

authorGroups.name: Transcendentalists
authorGroups.description: Transcendentalism is a philosophical, spiritual, and literary movement that developed in the late 1820s and 1830s in the New England region of the United States.

authorGroups.name: American Realists
authorGroups.description: American realism was a movement in art, music and literature that depicted contemporary social realities and the lives and everyday activities of ordinary people.

authorGroups.name: Lost Generation
authorGroups.description: The term 'Lost Generation' is traditionally attributed to Gertrude Stein and was then popularized by Ernest Hemingway in the epigraph to his novel The Sun Also Rises, and his memoir A Moveable Feast. It refers to a group of American literary notables who lived in Paris and other parts of Europe from the time period which saw the end of World War I to the beginning of the Great Depression.

authorGroups.name: Harlem Renaissance
authorGroups.description: African American poets, novelists, and thinkers, often employing elements of blues and folklore, based in the Harlem neighborhood of New York City in the 1920s.

authorGroups.name: Southern Agrarians
authorGroups.description: A group of Southern American poets, based originally at Vanderbilt University, who expressly repudiated many modernist developments in favor of metrical verse and narrative. Some Southern Agrarians were also associated with the New Criticism.

authorGroups.name: Beat Generation
authorGroups.description: An American movement of the 1950s and 1960s concerned with counterculture and youthful alienation.

authorGroups.name: Black Arts Movement
authorGroups.description: An African-American-led art and literary movement that was active during the 1960s and 1970s and related to Black Power politics.

authorGroups.name: New York School
authorGroups.description: Urban, gay or gay-friendly, leftist poets, writers, and painters of the 1960s.
